He has practitioner-level authority (Senior Manager, 3 months tenure, hired to transform procurement) but does NOT control budget, cannot navigate us to the Economic Buyer, and has no confirmed executive sponsorship or peer coalition. Current engagement: active. Risk: if Aurelien loses internal support, gets overruled by CFO, or a competitor offers a stronger champion path (e.g. exec sponsor intro), Zycus has no fallback relationship. Developing Aurelien into a strong champion requires: (1) arm him with a quantified business case for the CFO; (2) secure an invite to the CFO budget presentation or an exec-level value conversation; (3) multi-thread to Olaf Herrera, procurement leadership, or IT/finance stakeholders who will weigh in on the RFP shortlist.

competition.narrative: Competitive field is large and mostly opaque. Viva Aerobus is evaluating 10 vendors in parallel via RFI (Zycus is one of ten per Aurelien 22 May call: 'I have a list of 10 companies'). Nine competitors are unnamed; Zycus has zero visibility into their identity, pricing, or strengths. The known competition is: (1) Status quo (manual Excel/email processes + SAP S/4HANA native P2P functionality) — this is the incumbent 'do nothing' alternative and the strongest current threat because budget is unconfirmed and the CFO may reject the business case or approve only a minimal spend, forcing Viva to continue with manual workarounds. Aurelien stated 'it's all about the money at the end' and 'if we don't get the budget maybe we have to pick our battles' (calls 22 May, 08 Jun 2026), so if cost is too high the deal defaults to status quo. (2) iCue CLM (incumbent for contract lifecycle management, owned by legal, under implementation per Aurelien 08 Jun call). Aurelien said 'it's not working well' and 'CLM should be managed by procurement', but if legal defends their tool Zycus CLM may be blocked, reducing deal scope. (3) Nine unnamed RFI vendors (likely a mix of Coupa, SAP Ariba, Ivalua, GEP, Jaggaer, and regional/niche players) — all are live, active competitors receiving the same RFI with responses due 03 Jul 2026 and running demos in parallel. Threat level for the unnamed nine is UNKNOWN (cannot rank without visibility). How we win against status quo: Zycus must quantify the cost of inaction (manual FTE burden, maverick spend leakage, compliance/reputational risk, lost savings from reactive renewals) in the business case, position the Phase 1 quick-win approach to derisk adoption, and make the CFO conversation about ROI speed, not upfront cost. How we win against the RFI field: differentiate on (a) Microsoft Teams integration for adoption/change management (Aurelien explicitly valued this: 'easy enough… get away all that training piece'), (b) autonomous negotiation agent (Merlin Anna) for tail spend (positioned on 22 May call, Aurelien said 'I would love to try it' but deferred evaluation), (c) SAP S/4HANA certified connectors (go-live Feb 2026, integration is a hard requirement), and (d) phased implementation capability with quick wins (Aurelien stated 'start small and build upon it… claim those small wins' as a priority). Wedge: Zycus is the best-fitModular platform for a fast-growing mid-market airline that needs enterprise S2P capability without enterprise complexity or change-management friction, delivered in phases with Teams-native adoption and quick time-to-value. Competitive blind spot: without knowing the other nine vendors Zycus cannot tailor pricing, counter-position, or stress-test the wedge. This is a significant risk.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is acute, operational, and clearly owned by Aurelien Viaud (Indirect Procurement & Governance Senior Manager, hired 3 months ago explicitly to professionalize procurement per his 08 Jun intro). The organization grew rapidly (40 to 100+ aircraft in 5 years) with zero procurement infrastructure: decentralized buying, manual Excel supplier intake, email-driven RFQs, no spend visibility, no catalogs, no contract renewal alerts, no supplier risk monitoring, and all spend under $65k USD bypassing procurement entirely ('everything below 65k is totally delegated to the user area, we don't see it'). Specific pains stated by Aurelien: (1) 'spend analysis we have nothing… if you want to know something about the supplier… are we paying on time, what's the spend… it's a nightmare'; (2) 'there is no alerts in terms of what's expiring in next 3 months, I have no clue… we're trying to go from reactive when a supplier knocks out to a bit more proactive… we lost our opportunity to improve conditions'; (3) supplier compliance is fully manual, risk of reputational damage from 'suppliers where we have addresses that form too much nowhere' and no recurring checks ('recurring checks are not performed'); (4) maverick buying and PO after-the-fact: 'we have supplier performing jobs for the group and when we know about it is because we have to just fill the gap because we need to pay them, so we have to create a PO… that type of stuff we want to avoid because it's not good practice at all'; (5) CLM is broken: legal owns iCue CLM tool which 'is not working well… if I need something they take forever to have feedback… contracts should be managed by procurement'. Pain owner is Aurelien (operationally) but financial/budget pain owner is the CFO (unmapped). Pain is confirmed across calls and drives the full S2P RFI, but no quantified financial impact (cost, FTE, cycle time) has been calculated, weakening the CFO business case.

decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria are clearly articulated from discovery and RFI scope. Viva Aerobus is evaluating 10 vendors (including Zycus) on: (1) module coverage for full Source-to-Pay (supplier intake/risk/compliance, eProcurement, sourcing, spend analytics, contract lifecycle management, and invoicing in priority order); (2) user experience and change-management fit (Microsoft Teams integration, ease of adoption by young user base avg age 28–30, minimal training overhead); (3) SAP S/4HANA integration (go-live Feb 2026, need certified connectors and seamless data flows); (4) pricing structure and total cost (transaction-based vs user-based, budgetary estimate required, cost is the explicit gating factor per 'it's all about the money'); (5) phased implementation capability (ability to start small with Phase 1 quick wins and scale modularly); (6) supplier compliance automation (recurring risk checks, sanctions screening, reputational risk, Mexico-specific blacklist 'website' compliance); (7) speed to value (something up and running by end of 2026, 6-month target from RFI). Aurelien stated priorities explicitly: 'supplier intake, procure to pay, supplier relationship management, analytics, CLM… in that order' and 'we need agile while doing it… not be a burden for the whole organization' (call 08 Jun 2026). RFI questionnaire is 'already done, I have to add a few things' and includes 'high level costing questions' (call 08 Jun 2026). Criteria are practitioner-level; CFO's criteria (cost vs best-of-breed, risk appetite, vendor consolidation preference) are unknown.

ai_fit_signal.summary: Viva Aerobus is AI Curious. Aurelien explicitly stated 'AI is our friend' when describing how he used AI/Copilot to research and discover Zycus (22 May call), signaling openness to AI-driven solutions. The buyer values automation and ease-of-use (Teams integration resonated: 'easy enough, get away training piece'), and Aurelien expressed interest in the autonomous negotiation agent ('I would love to try it') but deferred deeper evaluation until later. The organization is young (avg user age 28–30 per Olaf Herrera), fast-moving, and growth-focused (40 to 100+ aircraft in 5 years), which correlates with higher AI adoption appetite. However, no quantified AI ROI case, no AI-specific use case identified in the RFI, and no exec-level AI strategy discussion has occurred. Viva is open to AI as an enabler of efficiency and agility but is not leading with AI as a strategic imperative. AI is a MEANS (automation, adoption ease) not the PRIMARY buying criterion (which is cost/budget and phased ROI). AIS Score/Status/Why fields are null in Salesforce, so no authoritative AI excitement score exists. Tier: AI Curious (open and interested, not resistant, but not AI-hungry or AI-native).

business_case.evidence: Qualitative pain confirmed and acute (manual Excel supplier intake, email-driven RFQs, zero spend visibility, reactive contract renewals, $65k-and-under maverick buying bypassing procurement, compliance/reputational risk from unvetted suppliers), but NO quantified financial impact (cycle time savings, FTE reduction, spend under management gain, cost avoidance, ROI payback). Aurelien stated pain clearly ('spend analysis we have nothing… it's a nightmare'; 'everything below 65k is delegated to user area, we don't see it'; 'no alerts for contract expiration… we lost our opportunity to improve conditions') but has not built a business case for the CFO. Aurelien stated multiple times 'it's all about the money' and the RFI is to gather cost data for the CFO budget request. No budget range, CFO appetite, or approval timeline has been confirmed. If Zycus is significantly more expensive than competitors or if the CFO approves a budget below the threshold for even a Phase 1 pilot, the deal stalls or loses.

Aurelien's goal to have 'something up and running by end of 2026' (stated 08 Jun call, ~6 months from now) may be unrealistic for a full S2P implementation, especially if budget approval is delayed past Aug 2026 and RFP, vendor selection, contracting, InfoSec, and implementation stretch into Q4 2026. A typical enterprise S2P implementation (even phased) takes 4–6 months from contract signature to go-live. If Zycus cannot deliver a Phase 1 quick win (e.g. supplier intake or eProcurement pilot) by Q4 2026, Aurelien may favor a faster competitor (e.g. a lightweight niche player with 60-day deployment) or delay the project into 2027, pushing close date and risking disqualification. The 19 Jun demo cancellation (by Aurelien, reason unstated) and the 323-day close date (18 May 2027) suggest the timeline may already be slipping or deprioritized. If the RFI-to-contract cycle drags past Oct 2026, the 'end of 2026' goal becomes unachievable and the deal may stall.

Single-threaded relationship with zero multi-threading or peer coalition. ai_positioning_strength.score: Moderate — AI capabilities positioned and buyer is receptive ('AI is our friend'), but not yet activated as competitive differentiation or tied to specific pain points
ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning is PRESENT but not yet activated as a competitive wedge. Zycus positioned two AI capabilities on the 22 May intro call: (1) Microsoft Teams conversational AI for intake (Merlin assistant guiding users through requests, routing to contracts/catalogs/sourcing, minimizing training overhead), which resonated strongly with Aurelien ('easy enough, get away training piece… you just get away all that training piece of it'); and (2) autonomous negotiation agent (Merlin Anna) for tactical tail-spend sourcing, which generated interest but was deferred for deeper evaluation ('I would love to try it… we have to take a look at that'). Aurelien explicitly stated 'AI is our friend' when describing how he discovered Zycus via AI/Copilot research, signaling openness to AI-driven automation. However, AI has not yet been positioned as a WEDGE against competitors or tied to Viva's specific pain points (e.g. 'autonomous agent handles the $65k-and-under tail spend that currently bypasses procurement entirely, reclaiming that spend under management without adding FTE burden'). The 30 Jun demo is the opportunity to activate AI as the wedge: demo the Teams intake flow live, show the autonomous agent negotiating a real Viva-relevant category (office supplies, MRO parts, janitorial services), and tie it directly to Aurelien's stated priorities (agility, low training overhead, quick wins, spend under management below $65k threshold). If AI is demo'd generically without tying to Viva's pain, it remains a feature, not a wedge.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong fit on functionality, user experience, and implementation approach; adoption/change-management and phased ROI are the primary buying criteria and Zycus's core differentiators. Budget and cost structure are the unknown: if Zycus pricing is significantly above the RFI field or the CFO approves minimal budget, fit weakens. Key alignments: (1) Microsoft Teams integration matches Aurelien's priority for adoption ease and minimal training overhead ('easy enough, get away training piece'); (2) phased implementation with quick wins matches Aurelien's stated preference ('start small, claim small wins, see value'); (3) SAP S/4HANA certified connectors match technical requirement (go-live Feb 2026); (4) full modular S2P suite matches long-term vision (supplier intake, eProcurement, sourcing, analytics, SRM, CLM in priority order). Partial exposure: (1) CLM module may conflict with legal's incumbent iCue tool (political risk); (2) pricing structure (transaction-based vs user-based) and total cost are unconfirmed, and Aurelien has not provided commercial data inputs for budgetary estimate despite Karson's 22 May offer ('if I can get those commercial data inputs I can give you a budgetary estimate').

deltas.subject: Multi-thread aggressively: zero buyer-side contact roles on the opp, and only Aurelien has engaged across two calls. value.detail: Aurelien's goal to have 'something up and running by end of 2026' (stated 08 Jun call, ~6 months from now) may be unrealistic for a full S2P implementation, especially if budget approval is delayed past Aug 2026 and RFP, vendor selection, contracting, InfoSec, and implementation stretch into Q4 2026. A typical enterprise S2P implementation (even phased) takes 4–6 months from contract signature to go-live. If Zycus cannot deliver a Phase 1 quick win (e.g. supplier intake or eProcurement pilot) by Q4 2026, Aurelien may favor a faster competitor (e.g. a lightweight niche player with 60-day deployment) or delay the project into 2027, pushing close date and risking disqualification. The 19 Jun demo cancellation (by Aurelien, reason unstated) and the 323-day close date (18 May 2027) suggest the timeline may already be slipping or deprioritized. If the RFI-to-contract cycle drags past Oct 2026, the 'end of 2026' goal becomes unachievable and the deal may stall.
